findings.title: Pure alias/model helpers duplicated verbatim between chat.js and manage.js
findings.problem: Six provider/model helpers are copy-pasted across the two frontends. CLAUDE_MODEL_OPTIONS, modelShortName, providerAliasTiers, normalizeModelForProvider and modelDisplayName are BYTE-IDENTICAL in body (chat.js 2079-2111/2246-2308 vs manage.js 1831-1844/1855-1890). providerAliasMap is near-identical logic but reads a different cache (_providerList in chat.js:2237 vs _providerData.providers in manage.js:1850) and chat.js additionally filters entries lacking .model. Any future tweak to alias display (e.g. the GLM5.2 display-name rule in modelDisplayName) must be applied twice and will silently drift, as it already has for providerAliasMap.
findings.recommendation: Create public/shared/models.js as a plain classic <script> (NOT an ES module) and load it via <script src='shared/models.js'></script> in chat.html (before chat.js, after i18n.js — line ~1395) and manage.html (before manage.js, line ~1362). This exactly mirrors the existing i18n.js + window.t pattern both files already depend on. Expose globals: CLAUDE_MODEL_OPTIONS, ALIAS_TIERS, modelShortName, aliasTiersFromMap(aliasMap), normalizeModelForAlias(aliasMap, model), modelDisplayNameFromMap(aliasMap, model), aliasTierLabel(tier, entry). The shared functions take the aliasMap OBJECT (not providerId), so each page keeps a 2-line local providerAliasMap(providerId) that looks up its own cache and returns the map — this is the minimal safe boundary that does NOT touch either cache. The 5 truly-identical helpers move verbatim; providerAliasMap stays page-local. ~40 lines of duplication eliminated; both pages keep their DOM/event/API behavior intact.
findings.failure_scenario: If only one copy of a shared helper (e.g. modelDisplayName) is updated to handle a new alias-tier shape, the other page shows the raw tier key 'opus' instead of the mapped display name 'GLM5.2' for alias-only relays.

findings.title: Tier list ['opus','sonnet','haiku','fable'] hardcoded 6 times across the two frontends
findings.problem: The alias-tier ordering literal ['opus','sonnet','haiku','fable'] is repeated 6 times: once in chat.js, five times in manage.js (in providerAliasTiers, in rebuildModelOptions, in readAliasMapFields, in fillAliasMapFields, and in the alias-map editor row builder). It is the de-facto contract with the server (src/providers.js:309 writes exactly these tiers). A new tier (or reorder) requires hunting all 6 sites; missing one desynchronizes the editor fields from the picker.
findings.recommendation: Export const ALIAS_TIERS = ['opus','sonnet','haiku','fable'] from the shared module (finding pure-alias-helpers-duplicated) and replace all 6 literals. Zero behavior change — pure constant substitution.

findings.title: Alias tier option label format string '${t} · ${name} · ${model}' copy-pasted 3 times
findings.problem: The exact template `${t}${m.name ? ` · ${m.name}` : ''} · ${m.model}` (rendering 'opus · GLM5.2 · glm-5.2') is inlined in chat.js modelChoiceLabel and TWICE in manage.js (inside showModelPicker optionList and inside rebuildModelOptions). A label-format change (separator, ordering, escaping) must be applied 3x and is easy to get wrong — note manage.js:2057 omits escaping on user-supplied m.model/m.name while chat.js:2282 also passes it through unescaped, so an XSS-via-alias-name fix would have to land in 3 places.
findings.recommendation: Extract aliasTierLabel(tier, entry) -> `${tier}${entry.name ? ` · ${entry.name}` : ''} · ${entry.model}` into the shared module and call it from all 3 sites. Optionally centralize escapeHtml handling at the same time.

findings.title: manage.js rebuildModelOptions reimplements providerAliasTiers inline instead of calling it
findings.problem: Inside its own file, manage.js duplicates the body of its own providerAliasTiers function: rebuildModelOptions (manage.js:2050-2052) writes `['opus','sonnet','haiku','fable'].filter(t => aliasMap[t] && aliasMap[t].model)` instead of calling providerAliasTiers. The two implementations must be kept in sync by hand. The reason for the inline copy is that providerAliasTiers takes a providerId and looks up _providerData.providers, while rebuildModelOptions already holds the prov object.
findings.recommendation: Once aliasTiersFromMap(aliasMap) exists in the shared module (taking the map directly), make both providerAliasTiers(providerId) and rebuildModelOptions call it. providerAliasTiers becomes `return aliasTiersFromMap(providerAliasMap(providerId))`; rebuildModelOptions becomes `const tiers = aliasTiersFromMap(prov && prov.aliasMap ? prov.aliasMap : null)`. Single source of truth.

findings.title: chat.js showModelPicker(current, providerOptions) is dead code and a near-duplicate of manage.js showModelPicker
findings.problem: chat.js defines showModelPicker at line 2169 but it has ZERO callers in chat.js or chat.html (grep shows only the definition; the model button path actually uses showAIConfigPicker at chat.js:2439). It is also a structural near-duplicate of manage.js showModelPicker (manage.js:1894) — same overlay/box/select/custom-input/buttons chrome (~40 overlapping lines) — but a LEGACY signature: chat.js's version takes (current, providerOptions), is NOT alias-tier aware, and lacks the Esc/Enter keyboard handling + select focus that manage.js has. Leaving 55 lines of dead, divergence-prone UI code risks someone wiring it up by accident and getting non-alias-aware behavior.
findings.recommendation: Delete chat.js:2169-2224 entirely after a final confirmation grep (no string/eval/indirect reference exists — verified). This removes half of the showModelPicker duplication for free with zero behavior risk, since the function is unreachable. Do NOT attempt to merge the two live showModelPicker signatures into one shared dialog in this round — chat.js's live dialog is showAIConfigPicker (a different 3-field provider+model+effort dialog, chat.js:2310), so a merge would touch DOM/keyboard/focus behavior across both pages and is MEDIUM risk for little remaining gain.

findings.title: Provider-list caches (_providerList vs _providerData) diverge by design — keep out of the shared boundary
findings.problem: A naive 'extract everything provider-related' pass would try to merge chat.js's _providerList + ensureProviderList(appType) with manage.js's _providerData = {available, providers, defaults, ccSwitchAvailable, stats}. They are NOT the same: chat.js only reads /api/providers?appType=... for the current cli and caches a flat array; manage.js caches the full provider-management payload (with defaults + cc-switch availability + CRUD stats) refreshed through a different code path. Merging would force one page to carry the other's fields, change fetch lifecycles, and risk the exact behavior change the constraints forbid.
findings.recommendation: Do NOT merge the caches. The correct shared-module boundary (per finding pure-alias-helpers-duplicated) exposes pure functions that take an aliasMap/provider OBJECT; each page keeps its own cache + its own 2-line providerAliasMap(providerId) lookup. This intentionally leaves _providerList and _providerData as page-local state. Document this so future refactors don't fold them together.

findings.title: applyWireDefaults() is dead code — defined but never called (logic was inlined into resolveSpawnEnv)
findings.problem: applyWireDefaults(env) is defined at src/providers.js:78-86 (with an 8-line block comment at 61-77 explaining its purpose) but has zero call sites anywhere in the repo. The only mentions of the name are in comments: the doc comment at line 10 and a comment at line 593 that explicitly says 'do NOT use applyWireDefaults which would inject claude-* names that relays like iFlytek reject with 10404'. The actual remap logic was reimplemented inline inside resolveSpawnEnv (src/providers.js:591-600), which promotes the relay's own tier var to ANTHROPIC_MODEL instead. The function is not exported, so it is purely dead. It is actively misleading: a reader could call it and reintroduce the 10404 bug it was abandoned for.
findings.recommendation: Delete the applyWireDefaults function (lines 78-86) and the now-stale portion of its explanatory comment block. Verify the inline logic at lines 591-600 stays as the single source of truth. No behavior change since it is never invoked.

findings.title: isAliasOnlyClaude() is dead code — defined but never called anywhere
findings.problem: isAliasOnlyClaude(cfg) is defined at src/providers.js:89-92 but grep across server.js, src/, public/, tests/, scripts/ finds zero references outside its own definition. The equivalent check is computed inline at src/providers.js:620 (`aliasOnly: !!env.ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL && !src.ANTHROPIC_MODEL`) and again inside the console.log at line 618. Not exported, never invoked — pure dead code that has already been superseded by inline expressions.
findings.recommendation: Delete the isAliasOnlyClaude function (lines 89-92) and its preceding comment (lines 87-88). The inline computation at line 620 already covers every callsite.

findings.title: getProviderRow is a dead back-compat alias (server.js no longer imports it)
findings.problem: src/providers.js:364-365 defines `const getProviderRow = getProvider;` with comment 'Back-compat alias (server.js used getProviderRow previously).' and exports it at line 829. A repo-wide grep for `getProviderRow` returns only this file's own definition + export (plus sibling worktree copies) — server.js, public/*, and tests/* contain zero references. The back-compat consumer no longer exists, so the alias and its export are dead.
findings.recommendation: Remove the `getProviderRow` alias (lines 364-365) and drop `getProviderRow,` from the module.exports block at line 829. Callers already use getProvider.

findings.title: providers.available() is an unused export (trivial `return true`, never imported)
findings.problem: src/providers.js:111 `function available() { return true; }` is exported at line 825 but is never imported or called by server.js, tests, scripts, or any other file. The comment at line 110 ('The store is always usable...') explains the `true` but nothing consumes the result. It is a vestigial API surface from before providers.json was always-present.
findings.recommendation: Delete the available function (line 111) and its preceding comment, and remove `available,` from module.exports (line 825). If a stable public API is desired for plugin compatibility, instead keep it but mark it explicitly as deprecated — but as-is it is unused.

findings.title: CODEX_HOMES_DIR is exported but never imported externally (only used internally)
findings.problem: The constant CODEX_HOMES_DIR (src/providers.js:57) is exported at line 840, but the only consumer is internal (line 624, inside resolveSpawnEnv). No file imports `providers.CODEX_HOMES_DIR`. The constant itself is live, but exposing it in the public export surface is dead and widens the module's API unnecessarily.
findings.recommendation: Keep the constant definition (line 57); only remove `CODEX_HOMES_DIR,` from module.exports (line 840). Internal usage at line 624 is unaffected.

findings.title: CLAUDE_MODEL_OPTIONS is byte-for-byte duplicated in chat.js and manage.js (5 hardcoded claude-* model ids in each)
findings.problem: The 7-entry CLAUDE_MODEL_OPTIONS array — containing the hardcoded wire ids 'claude-fable-5', 'claude-fable-5[1m]', 'claude-opus-4-8', 'claude-sonnet-4-6', 'claude-haiku-4-5-20251001' plus the ''/default and '__custom__' sentinels — is defined identically at public/chat.js:2079-2087 and public/manage.js:1831-1839 (diff confirms byte-identical). Adding, renaming, or version-bumping a model requires editing both files in lockstep; forgetting one leaves the chat picker and the manage-screen picker out of sync. This is the single most-impactful hardcoded-model-id hotspot: 10 literal occurrences of model ids across the two copies.
findings.recommendation: Extract the array (and the sibling modelShortName helper, which is also identical — see next finding) into a shared frontend module, e.g. public/shared/models.js exposing window.CLAUDE_MODEL_OPTIONS and window.modelShortName, loaded via a <script> tag in both pages. Both files then reference the single source. Pure data move; no behavior change.

findings.title: modelShortName() and providerAliasMap() are duplicated verbatim across chat.js and manage.js
findings.problem: modelShortName(model) is byte-identical at public/chat.js:2108 and public/manage.js:1841 (diff-confirmed). providerAliasMap(providerId) is likewise duplicated at public/chat.js:2235 and public/manage.js:1848 (same body). Both depend on the duplicated CLAUDE_MODEL_OPTIONS / _providerData respectively. Divergence risk: a fix to alias resolution in one picker won't propagate to the other.
findings.recommendation: Co-locate with the CLAUDE_MODEL_OPTIONS extraction (previous finding) in public/shared/models.js. Both helpers are pure (read-only over their arguments / a global cache) and move cleanly.

findings.title: CLAUDE_ROUTING_KEYS is re-implemented as an inline literal in server.js startup strip (comment warns it MUST stay in sync)
findings.problem: src/providers.js:509-526 defines the authoritative CLAUDE_ROUTING_KEYS array (ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL/AUTH_TOKEN/API_KEY/MODEL/SMALL_FAST_MODEL/DEFAULT_{OPUS,SONNET,HAIKU}_MODEL/CLAUDE_CODE_SIMPLE). server.js:23-29 re-hardcodes a hand-synced subset (the same 8 ANTHROPIC_* keys, minus CLAUDE_CODE_SIMPLE which is stripped in a separate loop at 45-51) as an inline array literal at startup. The comment at server.js:20 explicitly says 'MUST stay in sync with CLAUDE_ROUTING_KEYS in src/providers.js'. When providers.js adds a key (e.g. a future ANTHROPIC_DEFAULT_FABLE_MODEL), the server.js strip will silently miss it and leak the inherited value to spawned children — exactly the class of bug the surrounding comment already describes.
findings.recommendation: At server.js startup, import and reuse `providers.CLAUDE_ROUTING_KEYS` for the strip loop instead of the inline literal. CLAUDE_CODE_SIMPLE is already in CLAUDE_ROUTING_KEYS so the separate second loop (lines 45-51) can also be collapsed, or filtered out if a distinct log message is desired. This removes the manual sync obligation entirely.

findings.title: PROBE_CANDIDATES hardcodes 5 claude-* wire model ids as an inline literal
findings.problem: src/providers.js:784 `const PROBE_CANDIDATES = ['claude-sonnet-4-5', 'claude-opus-4-8', 'claude-haiku-4-5', 'claude-sonnet-4.5', 'claude-sonnet-5'];` is already a named const (good), but it is the only place in the backend where 5 distinct claude-* wire ids live as bare string literals with no documentation of what each represents or why both 'claude-sonnet-4-5' AND 'claude-sonnet-4.5' are present (they look like accidental duplicates but are intentionally different wire spellings some relays accept). When Anthropic ships the next model bump, this list must be hand-edited and the two near-duplicate sonnet spellings are a trap.
findings.recommendation: Add a one-line comment per candidate (or at minimum a block comment) explaining each — especially that 'claude-sonnet-4-5' (hyphen-version) and 'claude-sonnet-4.5' (dot-version) are BOTH probed because different relays accept different spellings. Optionally split into PROBE_CANDIDATES_CANONICAL and PROBE_CANDIDATES_ALIASES. No behavior change; this is documentation/constantization only.

findings.title: Probe SIGKILL timeout 25000ms is an unnamed magic number
findings.problem: src/providers.js:803 uses an inline `25000` for the per-candidate probe kill timeout: `setTimeout(() => { try { child.kill('SIGKILL'); } catch (_) {} }, 25000);`. Because probeRelayModels runs candidates sequentially (src/providers.js:820), this number is load-bearing: it bounds total probe latency to ~N×25s for the default 5 candidates (~2 minutes) before /api/providers/probe-relay returns. The rest of the codebase names such timeouts (DISPATCH_TIMEOUT_MS at server.js:1387, AUX_TIMEOUT_MS at server.js:4135, CLASSIFY_DELAY_MS/PROGRESS_SUMMARY_INTERVAL_MS at 5480-5481) but this one is inconsistent. The sibling output cap `2048` at line 800 and sample slice `95` at line 808 are also inline but are display-only and lower-value.
findings.recommendation: Define `const PROBE_TIMEOUT_MS = 25000;` near PROBE_CANDIDATES (line 784) and reference it at line 803. Pure rename; no behavior change.

reviews.dimension: public/chat.js global state management & reconnect races

findings.title: Reconnect refreshes _sessionProvider but guards _sessionEffectiveModel -> torn AI-config button
findings.problem: In the WS 'init' (reconnect) handler, _sessionProvider is written unconditionally (line 700: `if (msg.providerId !== undefined) _sessionProvider = msg.providerId || '';`) but _sessionEffectiveModel/_sessionModel are written only when empty (line 703: `if (msg.effectiveModel !== undefined && !_sessionEffectiveModel)`). loadSessionModel() does NOT re-run on reconnect (only called at line 3158 on first load), so on any reconnect the provider half of the button is refreshed from the server while the model half is frozen at whatever local last had. In a multi-tab/multi-device edit, or after a server-side alias-map change (effectiveSessionModel() in server.js:506 can return a different wire model while persisted.model stays the same), reconnect leaves the button showing NEW provider paired with the OLD model. The comment at line 698 ('loadSessionModel() 仅首次加载执行；重连只走 init') documents that the guard was added under the assumption REST re-runs on reconnect -- it does not -- so the guard only ever has the effect of freezing the model on reconnect. The asymmetric handling is the same class of bug as the recently-fixed _sessionProvider case, just on the sibling field.
findings.recommendation: Remove the `!_sessionEffectiveModel`/`!_sessionModel` guards at lines 703-706 so effectiveModel/model refresh from server init exactly like provider/effort already do. Preferably land this together with finding applyAIConfig-single-source so every field shares one explicit precedence rule. If a 'local pending pick wins' rule is genuinely wanted for next-turn overrides, apply it to provider too -- currently provider has no such protection, so the two fields follow different rules for no principled reason.

findings.title: _sessionProviderDisplayName guard prevents clearing on reconnect (empty-name falsy bug)
findings.problem: Line 701 writes `_sessionProviderDisplayName` with `if (msg.providerName)` -- a truthiness guard that is FALSE for '' and null. When the user switches the session to the default login (persisted.provider = ''), the server's init sends providerName: null (server.js:6760-6762/6803), the guard skips, and _sessionProviderDisplayName retains the PREVIOUS provider's display name forever. updateModelBtn (line 2116) prefers _sessionProviderDisplayName, so the button keeps showing the old provider name even though _sessionProvider was correctly cleared at line 700. This is byte-for-byte the same bug class as the recently-fixed _sessionProvider reconnect bug, on the adjacent line. Note the contrast right next to it: line 700 uses `!== undefined` + `|| ''` (correct clearing) and line 735 for the parallel _providerName also uses `!== undefined` -- only line 701 is wrong.
findings.recommendation: Mirror line 700 exactly: `if (msg.providerName !== undefined) _sessionProviderDisplayName = msg.providerName || '';`. One-line fix, no behavior change for the non-empty case.

findings.title: Five ad-hoc write sites re-derive the same AI-config globals with subtly different field handling (REST vs WS init vs 3x PATCH)
findings.problem: The per-session AI-config cluster (_sessionCli, _sessionProvider, _sessionProviderDisplayName, _sessionModel, _sessionEffectiveModel, _sessionEffort, _sessionEffectiveEffort) is mutated from FIVE separate sites with no shared helper: (1) loadSessionModel REST GET at 2411-2418; (2) WS 'init' handler at 693-705; (3) modelBtn/AI-config picker PATCH at 2453-2457; (4) effortBtn picker PATCH at 2477-2478; (5) providerBtn picker PATCH at 2578-2580. Each re-derives effectiveModel/effectiveEffort with slightly different fallbacks (e.g. `_sessionEffectiveModel = info.effectiveModel || info.model || ''` at 2416 vs `_sessionEffectiveModel = data.effectiveModel || data.model || ''` at 2455 vs the guarded WS path at 703-705), and each calls a different subset of update*Btn. The dual REST/WS source of truth has no defined precedence on first load either: loadSessionModel() at line 3158 is fire-and-forget (not awaited), so whether REST or the WS init lands last is timing-dependent -- currently harmless only because both read the same persisted state. This scattering is the structural root cause that made the recently-fixed _sessionProvider bug and the two high-severity bugs above possible: there is no single place that says 'this is how provider/model/effort are written'.
findings.recommendation: Extract `applyAIConfig({cli, provider, providerName, model, effectiveModel, effort, effectiveEffort})` that writes every field with ONE consistent rule (server-provided value wins, with identical empty-string fallbacks for ALL fields -- including the display name) and then calls a single refresh. Route all five sites plus the WS init through it. Separately, decide one explicit precedence rule (recommendation: WS init is authoritative on reconnect for the effective/running values, REST PATCH response is authoritative after an edit, REST loadSessionModel is authoritative on first load) and encode it in the call ordering rather than in scattered `!_sessionEffectiveModel`-style guards. This is the highest-value change in the dimension: it subsumes the two high-severity bugs above and prevents the next one.

findings.title: Two parallel global pairs hold the same provider identity with different clearing semantics
findings.problem: _providerId/_providerName (declared at 498-499, written at 734-735, read by updateContextBar at 1540) duplicate _sessionProvider/_sessionProviderDisplayName (declared at 2488-2489, written at 700-701, read by updateModelBtn at 2116-2117). Both pairs are fed from the SAME msg.providerId/msg.providerName on WS init, yet use different clearing rules: _providerName clears on empty via `!== undefined` (line 735), _sessionProviderDisplayName uses truthiness `if (msg.providerName)` and does NOT clear (line 701 -- the high-severity bug above). They can drift apart under any future code path that writes only one pair (e.g. the PATCH handlers at 2453/2578 update _sessionProvider but never touch _providerId/_providerName, so after a provider switch the context-bar label (1540) shows the OLD provider while the model button shows the NEW one until the next WS reconnect).
findings.recommendation: Collapse to one provider-identity pair. Have updateContextBar read from _sessionProvider/_sessionProviderDisplayName (the pair that is kept current by every PATCH handler), and delete _providerId/_providerName. If the context bar genuinely needs the 'last-spawned' provider distinct from the 'pending' provider, name the two pairs explicitly and document which message populates which -- but as written they are an accidental fork.

findings.title: updateModelBtn/updateProviderBtn/updateEffortBtn trio is dead complexity; providerBtn & effortBtn are permanently hidden
findings.problem: updateProviderBtn (2499-2503) and updateEffortBtn (2135-2139) both unconditionally set their button's display to 'none' and then call updateModelBtn. They exist only as click-anchors for the pickers, not as visible buttons. All visible state is rendered into modelBtn (2113-2123). The five write sites call these three functions in inconsistent combinations: loadSessionModel calls updateProviderBtn + updateModelBtn + updateEffortBtn (2414/2419/2420); WS init calls only updateEffortBtn + updateModelBtn (708-709, no updateProviderBtn); AI-config PATCH calls only updateModelBtn (2458); effort PATCH calls only updateEffortBtn (2479); provider PATCH calls updateProviderBtn + updateModelBtn (2581-2582). The inconsistency is currently MASKED because the hidden buttons' refresh is a visual no-op, but any future change that re-shows providerBtn or effortBtn will silently miss refreshes at several sites. (Also visible at 2579/2582: the provider-PATCH handler has 1-space-indented `_sessionModel = data.model || '';` and `updateModelBtn();` -- a copy-paste artifact that signals how ad-hoc these sites are.)
findings.recommendation: Replace the trio with a single updateAIConfigBtn() (the current body of updateModelBtn at 2113-2123) and have every write site call it. Keep providerBtn/effortBtn only as click-anchors (event listeners at 2435/2466/2566), not as display targets. Removes ~30 lines and eliminates the masked-inconsistency footgun.

findings.title: loadSessionModel() swallows all errors silently; transient REST failure leaves default AI config with no retry
findings.problem: loadSessionModel() ends with `} catch (_) {}` at line 2432, swallowing HTTP errors, parse errors, and network failures with no log, no banner, no retry. Because the WS init path only PARTIALLY repopulates the same globals (and, per the high-severity findings, guards/fails-to-clear some fields), a transient REST failure on first load means _sessionRole/_sessionMemory/_sessionStreaming/_sessionAutoContinue/_sessionAutoCommit/_sessionAutoDispatch stay at defaults and the AI-config cluster leans entirely on whatever WS init happened to set -- which was exactly the precondition that made the recently-fixed _sessionProvider reconnect bug visible to users.
findings.recommendation: At minimum `console.warn('loadSessionModel failed', _)`. Better: surface a non-blocking addSystemMsg and retry once after a short delay. Low-risk, removes a silent-failure mode that interacts badly with the WS-only reconnect path.

findings.title: WS init does not re-push streaming/autoCommit/autoDispatch/role/memory on reconnect (multi-tab staleness gap)
findings.problem: WS init (665-739) only re-pushes cli/provider/model/effort. _sessionStreaming, _sessionAutoContinue, _sessionAutoCommit, _sessionAutoDispatch, _sessionRole are written ONLY by REST loadSessionModel (2421-2431) and the PATCH handlers; they are never re-pushed by the server on reconnect (server.js init payload at 6793-6805 omits them). So if another tab (or cc-switch) changes any of these, this tab's toggles stay stale across reconnects until a full page reload. Only _sessionMemory has a dedicated WS event path (applyMemoryEvent at 2835). This is not a reconnect race within the AI-config cluster proper, but it is the same REST/WS dual-load pattern applied to the sibling session globals and could host the same class of bug.
findings.recommendation: Out of scope for this round but worth flagging: either include streaming/autoCommit/autoDispatch/role in the WS init payload (server.js:6793) and apply them in the init handler, OR trigger a REST loadSessionModel() on every reconnect (which would also resolve the high-severity effectiveModel/display-name bugs structurally). Do not address by scattering more ad-hoc writes -- do it via the applyAIConfig-style helper from finding applyAIConfig-single-source extended to cover the full session-config shape.

findings.title: applyWireDefaults is dead code; its body was inlined into resolveSpawnEnv
findings.problem: applyWireDefaults (lines 78-86) has zero call sites. The only references are two comments: the header at line 10 ('see applyWireDefaults in resolveSpawnEnv') and the inline comment at line 593 ('do NOT use applyWireDefaults...'). The actual alias-only model promotion now lives inline in resolveSpawnEnv at lines 591-600, so the function is dead but its name in the comments makes a reader believe it is still part of the spawn path. DEFAULT_TIER_MAP (mentioned in the task) is already gone from the main branch.
findings.recommendation: Delete applyWireDefaults (lines 78-86) and reword the two comments that reference it (line 10 header, line 593 inline) to describe the inline promotion logic instead. WIRE_DEFAULT_MODEL is still used (server.js:614) so keep that constant.

findings.title: isAliasOnlyClaude helper is defined but never called anywhere
findings.problem: isAliasOnlyClaude(cfg) (lines 89-92) is not exported (absent from module.exports at 824-843) and has no internal callers. grep across src/, server.js, and public/*.js returns only its definition. The alias-only check is instead performed inline in resolveSpawnEnv (line 591) and summarize (line 302). The helper is leftover from an earlier factoring.
findings.recommendation: Delete isAliasOnlyClaude (lines 89-92).

findings.title: getProviderRow export is an unused back-compat alias
findings.problem: getProviderRow (line 365, exported at 829) is declared as a back-compat alias of getProvider. Its own comment (line 364) says 'server.js used getProviderRow previously' (past tense), and grep finds no remaining callers in server.js or public/*.js. The export surface carries a dead symbol.
findings.recommendation: Drop getProviderRow from module.exports and delete the alias line.

findings.title: Wire-default docs and inline spawn logic disagree; the justifying comment misattributes the iFlytek case
findings.problem: The header comment (lines 61-71) and applyWireDefaults docstring (lines 73-77) state that a pure-passthrough alias-only relay with NO tier target (e.g. CrazyRouter) falls back to WIRE_DEFAULT_MODEL (claude-sonnet-4-5). The actual spawn path (lines 591-600) only promotes a tier target and does NOTHING when no tier target exists, dropping the documented fallback. Additionally the comment at lines 593-594 ('do NOT use applyWireDefaults which would inject claude-* names that relays like iFlytek reject') is factually wrong: applyWireDefaults uses the tier target when one exists (lines 80-85: tierTarget || WIRE_DEFAULT_MODEL), so for iFlytek (which HAS tier targets) it would inject astron-code-latest, NOT a claude-* name — identical to the inline code. The only real behavioral difference is the CrazyRouter no-tier case, which is undocumented. A maintainer reading this cannot tell whether the missing fallback is intended or a regression. Separately, the PROBE_CANDIDATES comment at lines 782-783 ('All Anthropic-compatible relays accept claude-* names') contradicts the central iFlytek premise (iFlytek rejects claude-* with 10404).
findings.recommendation: Reconcile docs with code in one pass: (1) decide whether the CrazyRouter no-tier WIRE_DEFAULT_MODEL fallback should happen at spawn (header says yes, code says no) and either restore it explicitly in resolveSpawnEnv or update the header; (2) correct the line 593-594 comment to state the real reason (avoid the claude-* fallback for passthrough relays / drop it entirely), not the iFlytek misattribution; (3) fix the PROBE_CANDIDATES comment so it no longer claims all relays accept claude-*. Comment-only changes — no behavior change required.

findings.title: WIRE_DEFAULT_MODEL is exported only so server.js can read an internal constant
findings.problem: WIRE_DEFAULT_MODEL is exported (line 841) for a single consumer: server.js providerDefaultModel (line 614) returns providers.WIRE_DEFAULT_MODEL when a provider summary is alias-only. The wire-default model name is an internal providers.js concern; exposing the constant invites server.js to branch on provider-internal state (p.aliasOnly). This is a leaky abstraction.
findings.recommendation: Encapsulate: either add a small providers.safeWireModel() accessor, or have getProviderSummary surface a 'defaultModel' field (already computed as p.model || p.modelOptions[0], with the aliasOnly→wire-default branch inside providers.js). Then drop WIRE_DEFAULT_MODEL from the exports.

findings.title: server.js re-implements parseConfig inline because the helper is private
findings.problem: parseConfig (lines 114-117) normalizes a settingsConfig that may be a string or object. It is used internally (e.g. lines 285, 393, 464) but not exported, so server.js:4711 re-implements the same expression inline: `typeof p.settingsConfig === 'string' ? JSON.parse(p.settingsConfig) : (p.settingsConfig || {})`. This couples server.js to the settingsConfig storage shape (it must know the value may be a JSON string).
findings.recommendation: Export parseConfig from providers.js and use it at server.js:4711. Better still, expose a `getProviderEnv(appType, id)` helper so server.js never touches the raw settingsConfig at all — but exporting parseConfig is the minimal low-risk fix.

findings.title: Wire-model selection logic is duplicated in two server.js spawn sites that consume providers spawn-env fields
findings.problem: The decision that turns resolveSpawnEnv/buildChildEnv output (providerModel, providerModels, skipDefaultModel) plus the session into the concrete --model arg is duplicated verbatim in server.js buildChatSpawnArgs (lines 805-816) and the chat-stream spawn (lines 6593-6600). The second copy even prefixes locals with '_' and the comment at 6593 admits 'Same wire-model resolution as buildChatSpawnArgs'. The logic is tightly coupled to the spawn-env contract that providers.js owns, yet lives in server.js twice. This is the highest-value clarity win in this area.
findings.recommendation: Extract a pure helper, e.g. providers.resolveWireModel(session, spawnEnv, fallbackModel) (honoring session.model only when it is an alias or a served model, else spawnEnv.providerModel, else fallback gated by skipDefaultModel), and call it from both server.js:811 and server.js:6595. Pure function over the spawn-env fields — low behavioral risk, removes the `_`-prefixed shadow copy.

findings.title: Claude alias tier keys are listed in three separate hand-maintained constants
findings.problem: The four ANTHROPIC_DEFAULT_{OPUS,SONNET,HAIKU,FABLE}_MODEL keys appear in three independent declarations: ALIAS_TIER_KEYS (lines 193-198, the canonical map), the inline array `aliasKeys` inside summarize (line 293), and PROBE_STRIP_KEYS (line 788, tier keys plus ANTHROPIC_MODEL + ANTHROPIC_SMALL_FAST_MODEL). CLAUDE_ROUTING_KEYS (line 509) also enumerates a related superset. Adding a fifth tier (or renaming one) requires editing all sites in lockstep.
findings.recommendation: Have summarize derive its aliasKeys from Object.values(ALIAS_TIER_KEYS), and build PROBE_STRIP_KEYS from the same source plus the extras. Keep one canonical list.

findings.title: summarize codex branch reinvents uniqueModels inline
findings.problem: summarize (lines 284-345, ~60 lines) is the fattest pure helper here and carries two appType branches. Its codex branch (lines 320-328) hand-rolls a Set-based dedup loop that is exactly what the existing uniqueModels helper (lines 130-138) does; the claude branch already calls uniqueModels at line 297. The function is also doing two jobs (extract fields + compute modelOptions/aliasMap), which blurs its single responsibility.
findings.recommendation: Replace the codex-branch dedup loop with uniqueModels([model, ...extras]). Optionally split the claude/codex field-extraction into two private helpers so summarize is a dispatch + shape call.

findings.title: getProviderUsageStats reads sessions.json directly, coupling providers.js to the sessions schema
findings.problem: The provider module declares SESSIONS_FILE (line 647) and reads sessions.json inside getProviderUsageStats (lines 708-717) to map session id -> provider id. sessions.json is server.js's persistence domain; reading it here couples providers.js to the sessions record shape (s.id, s.provider) and to the file location. More broadly, getProviderUsageStats + readDailyWindows + dateKey (lines 652-778) are token-accounting concerns that are not really provider-config concerns.
findings.recommendation: Flag only — do NOT extract this round (per the constraint against splitting into many files). For a future round: move readDailyWindows/getProviderUsageStats/dateKey into a sibling src/usage.js, and have the caller pass the session->provider map in so providers.js stops reading sessions.json directly.

findings.title: resolveSpawnEnv returns objects with inconsistent key sets across its five return paths
findings.problem: resolveSpawnEnv has five return points with different shapes: the no-providerId (562) and not-found (565) returns omit tools and codexHome; the claude happy-path (620) sets tools but not codexHome; the codex happy-path (637) sets codexHome but not tools; the codex catch (639) sets neither. buildChildEnv (line 553) then reads spawn.tools unconditionally (tolerating undefined). Functionally safe today, but brittle and confusing — a caller that destructures codexHome/tools silently gets undefined depending on which branch fired, and adding a field requires editing five sites.
findings.recommendation: Route all returns through one shape, e.g. a small `spawnResult(env, {appType, ...})` builder that always sets every key (tools/codexHome defaulting to undefined). Pure structural cleanup.

findings.title: The load-bearing 'is alias? served? else providerModel' block is byte-identical in two server.js spawn paths, held together only by a 'keep in sync' comment
locations: src/providers.js:192-197 (ALIAS_TIER_KEYS, the vocabulary source of truth)
findings.problem: buildChatSpawnArgs and runChatTurnStreaming each independently reimplement the exact same wire-model decision: `const isAlias = /^(opus|sonnet|haiku|fable|default)$/$/i.test(model); const served = providerModels || []; const hasProvider = providerModel != null; const model = !hasProvider ? (model || (skipDefaultModel ? null : claudeDefaultModel())) : ((model && (isAlias || served.includes(model))) ? model : providerModel)`. The second copy (server.js:6594) even carries the comment 'Same wire-model resolution as buildChatSpawnArgs' — a manual keep-in-sync marker. This block is the most bug-prone code in the repo right now: recent commits 6eedb11 and 74054b6 were literally model-id promotion bugs, and a divergence between the two copies silently sends the wrong `--model` to the claude CLI (relay 400 / 1211 / 10404). Both copies consume the same upstream values (providerModel/providerModels/skipDefaultModel produced by providers.resolveSpawnEnv), so they have identical inputs.
findings.recommendation: Extract one pure function into src/providers.js (which already owns providerModel/providerModels and the alias vocabulary) and call it from both sites: `resolveSessionWireModel(sessionModel, { providerModel, providerModels, skipDefaultModel })`. Build the alias regex inside it from `Object.keys(ALIAS_TIER_KEYS)` plus 'default' instead of a literal. Signature matches exactly what both call sites already have in scope, so each call site collapses to `const model = providers.resolveSessionWireModel(session.model, opts)` / `...(persisted.model, { providerModel, providerModels, skipDefaultModel })`. Delete the 'keep in sync' comment. This is the single source of truth for the alias-resolution POLICY on the server.

findings.title: CLAUDE_MODEL_OPTIONS + 5 alias helpers are duplicated character-for-character between chat.js and manage.js
findings.problem: Six frontend helpers and the CLAUDE_MODEL_OPTIONS constant are duplicated verbatim across the two browser bundles. The ONLY difference is the provider-list source: chat.js looks up `_providerList.find(o => o.id === providerId)`, manage.js looks up `(_providerData.providers || []).find(o => o.id === providerId)`. Every other line is identical, including the tier-order literal, the filter predicate, the display-name fallback ladder, and the i18n labelKey handling. The recent diff (label format `opus · GLM5.2 · glm-5.2`) had to be applied in both files at three call sites each — exactly the drift risk duplication creates.
findings.recommendation: Create public/model-utils.js loaded via <script src="model-utils.js"></script> BEFORE chat.js / manage.js (precedent: both chat.html:1377 and manage.html:1359 already load i18n.js this exact way). Move CLAUDE_MODEL_OPTIONS, modelShortName, providerAliasTiers, normalizeModelForProvider, modelDisplayName into it. Make them take the provider OBJECT (not providerId) so no page-specific array lives inside the shared file — e.g. `providerAliasTiers(provider)` reads `provider && provider.aliasMap`. Each page keeps only a one-line `getProviderById(id)` that finds from its own `_providerList` / `_providerData.providers`, then passes the result in. chat.js keeps its chat-only buildModelChoices/modelChoiceLabel which compose the shared helpers.

findings.title: The alias tier vocabulary ['opus','sonnet','haiku','fable'] is hardcoded in 8+ places instead of derived from the existing ALIAS_TIER_KEYS source of truth
findings.problem: The tier set {opus, sonnet, haiku, fable} already has ONE canonical owner — `ALIAS_TIER_KEYS` in providers.js:192. But every consumer re-types it: the regex appears twice in server.js, the literal `['opus','sonnet','haiku','fable']` appears 6 times across the two frontends, and src/providers.js:293 even re-declares the same 4 env keys as a parallel `aliasKeys` array instead of `Object.values(ALIAS_TIER_KEYS)`. Adding/removing a tier (or a future 'opus-pro') means hunting 10+ sites and hoping no regex is missed.
findings.recommendation: Two cheap constantizations. (1) Server: in providers.js expose `const ALIAS_TIERS = Object.keys(ALIAS_TIER_KEYS)` and an `isAliasModel(s)` built from `[...ALIAS_TIERS, 'default'].join('|')`; replace the line-293 `aliasKeys` with `Object.values(ALIAS_TIER_KEYS)`; the extracted server helper from F1 consumes both. (2) Frontend: since /api/providers already ships each provider's `aliasMap` (built in summarize() at src/providers.js:305-309), the shared model-utils.js should derive tiers from `Object.keys(provider.aliasMap)` ordered against a single `TIER_ORDER = ['opus','sonnet','haiku','fable']` constant rather than each call site re-typing it. That collapses 6 literals to 1.

findings.title: applyWireDefaults and isAliasOnlyClaude are dead code after the recent refactor, and a comment describing applyWireDefaults's old behavior is now stale
locations: src/providers.js:78-86 (applyWireDefaults definition — 0 call sites, not exported)
src/providers.js:89-92 (isAliasOnlyClaude definition — 0 call sites, not exported)
src/providers.js:593-595 (stale comment 'do NOT use applyWireDefaults which would inject claude-* names')
src/providers.js:586-597 (resolveSpawnEnv inlines the NEW promotion instead)
findings.problem: After the current diff, resolveSpawnEnv inlines its own tier-promotion (src/providers.js:586-597) and no longer calls applyWireDefaults. Confirmed: grep finds applyWireDefaults only at its definition (78) and two comment references (10, 593); it is not in module.exports (824-842). isAliasOnlyClaude (89) has zero references anywhere. Worse, the comment at 593 says 'do NOT use applyWireDefaults which would inject claude-* names' — but the CURRENT applyWireDefaults (78-86) promotes the tier target; it does NOT inject claude-* names. The comment describes the pre-diff behavior, so a future reader is warned about a hazard that no longer exists in that function. This is the classic 'duplication + incomplete edit' scar: the inline block in resolveSpawnEnv IS a second copy of applyWireDefaults's job.
findings.recommendation: Either (a) delete applyWireDefaults, isAliasOnlyClaude, and the stale 593 comment outright (they have no callers), updating the header comment at line 10 — lowest risk; or (b) if the intent was to keep one named helper, re-route resolveSpawnEnv's inline block (592-597) through applyWireDefaults and delete the inline copy, fixing the precedence inconsistency flagged separately. Prefer (a) for this round since the inline version is the live, tested path.

findings.title: manage.js rebuildModelOptions re-inlines the tier-build + label logic instead of reusing its own providerAliasTiers helper
locations: public/manage.js:1855-1861 (providerAliasTiers — the existing helper it duplicates)
public/manage.js:1876-1889 (modelDisplayName — could supply the label too)
findings.problem: rebuildModelOptions (the provider-edit form dropdown builder) manually does `const tiers = aliasMap ? ['opus','sonnet','haiku','fable'].filter(t => aliasMap[t] && aliasMap[t].model) : []` and then `opts = tiers.map(t => { const m = aliasMap[t]; return { value: t, label: `${t}${m.name ? ` · ${m.name}` : ''} · ${m.model}` }; })`. Both pieces already exist as helpers in the SAME file: providerAliasTiers (1855) produces exactly that filtered tier list, and the label format matches modelChoiceLabel/chat.js:2276. So manage.js duplicates its own helpers intra-file. The recent diff had to touch this site separately from the providerAliasTiers-based sites.
findings.recommendation: Rewrite the alias branch of rebuildModelOptions to use the existing helpers: `const tiers = providerAliasTiers(selectedProviderId)` (passing the resolved provider), then `opts = tiers.map(([t, m]) => ({ value: t, label: `${t}${m.name ? ` · ${m.name}` : ''} · ${m.model}` }))`. If F4's shared model-utils.js lands, this label builder becomes a shared `tierLabel(t, m)` too and both rebuildModelOptions and chat.js modelChoiceLabel collapse to it.

findings.title: Two copies of 'promote a tier target to ANTHROPIC_MODEL' pick the tier in different orders (opus-first vs sonnet-first)
locations: src/providers.js:593-596 (resolveSpawnEnv inline: SONNET → OPUS → HAIKU → FABLE)
findings.problem: applyWireDefaults picks the canonical model as `ANTHROPIC_DEFAULT_OPUS_MODEL || ...SONNET... || ...HAIKU... || ...FABLE`. The live resolveSpawnEnv inline block picks `ANTHROPIC_DEFAULT_SONNET_MODEL || ...OPUS... || ...HAIKU... || ...FABLE`. So which real model id becomes ANTHROPIC_MODEL for an alias-only relay that sets differing tier targets is decided differently by the two copies. Today it is latent because applyWireDefaults is dead code (F4) and most relays set all tiers to the same id, but it is a latent correctness trap if applyWireDefaults is ever revived or the inline block is refactored to call it without re-checking.
findings.recommendation: Decide the precedence once. Pick one (sonnet-first matches the CLI's default-tier bias and is the live behavior) and put it in a single `pickTierTarget(env)` helper that both the inline promotion and any future applyWireDefaults call. Cleanest when combined with F2's deletion: delete applyWireDefaults so only the sonnet-first inline path remains, and document the chosen precedence in its comment.

findings.title: Server alias regex includes 'default' but frontend tier vocabulary does not; the two runtimes cannot share a module but currently share no spec either
locations: public/chat.js:2249 / public/manage.js:1858 (tier list omits 'default'; uses '' for follow-default)
src/providers.js:289-339 summate() / 305-309 (aliasMap already shipped to clients via /api/providers)
findings.problem: Node (server.js, CommonJS) and browser (chat.js/manage.js, script tags) genuinely cannot share a require()'d module, so the alias-resolution policy is *correctly* implemented twice — but with no shared contract keeping them aligned beyond convention. There is also a real asymmetry: the server treats the literal string 'default' as an alias pass-through (server.js:811 regex), while the frontends never offer 'default' as a tier — they use '' (empty) to mean 'follow default', which the server then routes via skipDefaultModel/claudeDefaultModel(). Benign today, but it means 'what counts as an alias' has two slightly different definitions across runtimes, and nothing enforces they stay in sync.
findings.recommendation: Make the server the single source of the alias CONTRACT that the frontend already consumes: /api/providers (summarize() in src/providers.js:289) already ships each provider's `aliasMap`. (1) Add an ordered `tiers: ['opus','sonnet','haiku','fable']` field (derived from ALIAS_TIER_KEYS) to that summary so the frontend stops hardcoding the list at all — it iterates `provider.tiers`. (2) Cross-reference the two runtimes with a comment at server.js:811 and in model-utils.js naming /api/providers aliasMap+tiers as the contract. Do NOT try to share actual JS between Node and the browser — keep the implementations parallel but pointed at the same server-owned vocabulary. No HTTP API shape changes (tiers is an additive field on the existing provider summary object).

findings.title: server.js (7618 lines) bundles ~30 distinct cohesive responsibility regions; structure is undocumented
findings.problem: server.js is a god-file mixing unrelated domains. Survey of major cohesive regions (approx line ranges): (1) startup env-scrub + requires [1-89]; (2) auth/cookie helpers + login routes + auth middleware [90-229]; (3) port selection [231-407]; (4) CLI binary resolution + model/effort resolution [413-757]; (5) tmux recovery + codex session lookup [879-987]; (6) worktree/merge-state helpers [988-1012]; (7) directory git-readiness + directory/session persistence + worktree init [1013-1235]; (8) dispatch/ultracode/gateway control plane [1250-1614]; (9) session id allocation + createSession [1615-1869]; (10) the REST API mega-block: ~2860 lines spanning session/dashboard/agent-resource/directory-CRUD/memo/session-lifecycle/share/history/triggers/file/upload/voice/env-settings/VAPID/push/power [1870-4730]; (11) push-monitor + push dispatch [3964-4131]; (12) AuxQueue + aux routes [4133-4461]; (13) goal-config helpers + routes [4462-4602]; (14) provider-defaults state + provider routes [4603-4730]; (15) SPA/static serving [4732-4828]; (16) token-usage tracking [4829-4930]; (17) chat-history load/save/append [4931-5030]; (18) chat broadcast + provider token broadcast [5032-5099]; (19) bridge routers mount [5087-5130]; (20) classify/summary + session-memory helpers [5130-5314]; (21) workspace WS + notes + events [5315-5483]; (22) intent-classify scheduling + turn outcome/notify + progress summary [5483-5726]; (23) role-prompt + memory keyword/relevance [5727-5858]; (24) applyClaudeChatEvent [5859-5952]; (25) runChatTurn (535 lines, the worst single offender) [5953-6487]; (26) wait API + run-detached API [6488-6580]; (27) streaming turn [6581-6700]; (28) handleChatWs (243 lines) [6701-6943]; (29) WS hub dispatcher [6944-7150]; (30) triggers region (file-watch/cron/validate + pure glob helpers) [7178-7355]; (31) skill sync [7357-7514]; (32) startup orchestration + graceful shutdown [7515-7618]. This map is the prerequisite for any targeted extraction; recorded here as context for the actionable findings below.
findings.recommendation: Do NOT attempt a full split this round (high risk to behavior preservation). Instead, use this map to guide the small extractions in the other findings, and as the roadmap for future rounds (each region is a candidate src/ module: e.g. src/auth.js, src/token-usage.js, src/aux-queue.js, src/skill-sync.js, src/triggers.js). Add a brief region index as a comment block at the top of server.js so the next reader doesn't have to re-derive it.

findings.title: findAvailablePort is defined 5 times verbatim (235-407); 4 copies are pure dead code
findings.problem: Lines 235-267, 270-302, 305-337, 340-372 and 375-407 each declare `async function findAvailablePort(startPort)` with byte-identical bodies (verified: all five blocks md5 to eda34e707860db65d960cac75c6abcbf). JS function hoisting means only the LAST declaration (375-407) takes effect, so the first four are unreachable dead code — ~140 lines of it. The function is referenced exactly once, at line 7595 (`PORT = await findAvailablePort(PORT)`). This is the single largest zero-risk cleanup in the file.
findings.recommendation: Delete four of the five copies, keeping the line-375 definition. Behavior is byte-identical (all five are the same and only the last is live). Follow-up: the whole 32-line helper plus its .env writeback is a clean candidate for src/port.js later, but for this round just remove the duplicates.

findings.title: clampInt is defined twice with subtly different bodies (4462 vs 7178)
findings.problem: `function clampInt` is declared at line 4462 (`if (!Number.isFinite(n)) n = dflt; return Math.max(lo, Math.min(hi, n))`) and again at line 7178 (`if (Number.isNaN(n)) return dflt; return Math.max(min, Math.min(max, n))`). Only the second wins globally; the first is dead. For parseInt output the two are behaviorally equivalent (parseInt returns NaN or a finite integer, never ±Infinity), so the duplication is harmless today but is a latent trap if anyone edits the 'live' copy and assumes the first is in force. There is exactly one clampInt in the file that should exist.
findings.recommendation: Delete the line-4462 copy. Optionally co-locate the surviving clampInt with the trigger helpers that use it (or move to a shared src/util.js) so future readers find one definition, not two.

findings.title: The 'iterate WS clients and JSON.stringify-send' loop is copy-pasted 8+ times
findings.problem: The pattern `for (const c of clients) if (c.readyState === WebSocket.OPEN) { try { c.send(JSON.stringify(payload)); } catch(_) {} }` appears at lines 1785-1789, 1813-1817, 1835-1839, 2889-2893, 2972-2976, 4005-4009 (terminalBroadcast), 4371-4375 (auxQueue.broadcast), 5042-5046 (chatBroadcast), and 5319-5321 (workspaceBroadcast). Each copy is a drift hazard: e.g. the three named wrappers swallow send errors with try/catch but the four inline terminal loops at 1785/1813/1835/2972 do NOT, so a thrown send (rare but possible on a half-closed socket) would crash the data handler instead of being swallowed. That inconsistency is already a latent bug, and any new broadcast site will copy whichever neighbor it sees.
findings.recommendation: Add one helper near the WS hub, e.g. `function wsSendAll(clientSet, payload) { const json = JSON.stringify(payload); for (const c of clientSet) if (c && c.readyState === WebSocket.OPEN) { try { c.send(json); } catch (_) {} } }` and route all nine sites through it. This preserves behavior (and quietly fixes the missing try/catch on the four terminal loops), shrinks each call site to one line, and makes a future 'broadcast to all hubs' change a single-point edit.

findings.title: WS connection handler re-implements isLocalRequest + cookie auth inline instead of calling the shared auth helpers
findings.problem: The auth helpers (signToken 96, generateAuthCookie 100, verifyAuthCookie 105, parseCookies 111, isExternalProxy 121, isLocalRequest 130, isAuthenticated 135) are a clean self-contained group that reads only the module-level ACCESS_TOKEN. But the WS hub dispatcher at lines 6961-6965 re-implements isLocalRequest and the cookie-token check by hand: `const ip = req.socket.remoteAddress; const isLocal = (ip===127.0.0.1||...) && !isExternalProxy(req); const cookies = parseCookies(...); const hasCookie = cookies.multicc_auth && verifyAuthCookie(...)`. The only reason not to call isLocalRequest(req) is that the WS path uses req.socket.remoteAddress instead of express's req.ip/req.connection. So the localhost rule now lives in two places that must be kept in sync by hand (e.g. adding a new loopback range or proxy suffix requires editing both).
findings.recommendation: Extract a single `wsIsAuthenticated(req, urlObj)` helper next to the existing auth group that takes the raw socket address and searchParams, and have the WS dispatcher (6960-6970) call it. This centralizes the localhost+cookie+token rule with the HTTP isAuthenticated so the two cannot drift. (Optional, slightly larger: move the whole auth group to src/auth.js exporting {isAuthenticated, wsIsAuthenticated, signToken, ...} with ACCESS_TOKEN passed in — but that's a separate M finding, see auth-extract.)

findings.title: Self-contained pure helpers (glob matchers, memory/keyword/text utils, classify parser) are entangled with stateful chat code
findings.problem: Several groups of fully-pure functions (no fs/net/state, no HTTP coupling) are buried mid-file between stateful regions, making them hard to reason about and impossible to unit-test in isolation: (a) trigger glob matchers globToRegex/matchGlob/matchAnyGlob + _globCache [7219-7240]; (b) session-memory pure transforms getMemoryEntries/_memoryEvictionRank/_trimMemoryEntries/_memorySimilarity/_mergeMemoryEntries [5175-5240] (only read the constants SESSION_MEMORY_MAX 5162 and MEMORY_EVICTION_ORDER 5170); (c) text/keyword utils extractKeywords [5771-5791], getRelevantMemoryEntries [5797-5832], briefTaskDescription [5587-5595], and the classifier-output parser parseClassifyResult [5130-5145]. Each of these is deterministic and could be lifted verbatim. Their current placement makes the surrounding stateful logic (runChatTurn, appendChatMessage, fireTrigger) look larger and more tangled than it is.
findings.recommendation: Move group (a) to a new tiny src/glob.js (or src/text-utils.js) and groups (b)+(c) to a new src/memory-text.js, taking the two constants as module locals. Each move is mechanical: pure functions, no this/global references, no require changes beyond adding the import. Result: ~150 lines out of server.js, the helpers become unit-testable, and the stateful functions that remain visibly shrink. This is the lowest-risk 'real' extraction available — no HTTP, no persistence, no spawn.

findings.title: Cookie/auth helper cluster (7 functions + ACCESS_TOKEN) is a clean cohesive module trapped at the top of server.js
findings.problem: Lines 90-146 hold a self-contained auth subsystem: ACCESS_TOKEN (the only state), signToken, generateAuthCookie, verifyAuthCookie, parseCookies, isExternalProxy, isLocalRequest, isAuthenticated. It depends only on Node 'crypto' and an Express-like req shape. It is consumed by the login routes (153-202), the auth middleware (205-228), the access-token settings route (3908-3923, which hot-reloads ACCESS_TOKEN at runtime via a closure over the module-level let), and the WS hub (6961-6965). Because it lives in server.js it cannot be reused by the already-extracted src/ modules (share.js, detached.js each end up re-deriving token checks) and cannot be unit-tested in isolation. The runtime hot-reload contract (ACCESS_TOKEN is a `let` reassignable at runtime, and signToken/isAuthenticated read it live) is currently enforced only by comment [90-92] — moving to a module that exports getToken/setToken preserves it explicitly.
findings.recommendation: Create src/auth.js exporting { getAccessToken, setAccessToken, signToken, generateAuthCookie, verifyAuthCookie, parseCookies, isExternalProxy, isLocalRequest, isAuthenticated } (setAccessToken is called by the /api/settings/access-token route to keep the hot-reload working). server.js keeps a thin `const auth = require('./src/auth')` and destructures what it needs. Risk is low because the group has no other server.js state coupling; the only subtlety is preserving the live-read of ACCESS_TOKEN, which the getter/setter pair makes explicit. Pairs naturally with the wsIsAuthenticated helper in the ws-auth-inlines finding.

findings.title: Larger cohesive regions (AuxQueue, token-usage, skill-sync, provider-defaults, runChatTurn split) noted as low-priority / future
findings.problem: Beyond the low-risk findings above, several regions are cohesive enough to extract but carry MEDIUM risk this round and should be deferred: (a) AuxQueue object [4155-4388, ~230 lines] — a self-contained subsystem but coupled to loadChatHistory/appendChatMessage/persistedSessions/CLAUDE_CMD/providers/its own WS client set; extraction pattern exists (mirrors src/share.js, src/detached.js) but needs dependency injection. (b) token-usage tracking [4829-4930, ~100 lines] — touches persistedSessions (provider resolution) + file I/O; note naming would collide with existing src/token-global.js so it would become src/token-usage.js. (c) skill-sync [7357-7514, ~160 lines] — mostly pure fs/path but the skillConv.onAiConvertNeeded callback shells out via curl+run-detached; could extend the existing src/skills.js. (d) provider-defaults state [4597-4613] — tiny but has a circular-ish dep on the providers module. (e) the 535-line runChatTurn [5953-6487] is the single worst function but splitting it risks turn-lifecycle regressions across claude/codex/cancel/error/streaming paths. (f) the 2860-line REST mega-block [1870-4730] and the full server.js split are explicitly OUT OF SCOPE per the hard constraints.
findings.recommendation: Defer (a)-(e) to a later round after the S/M low-risk findings land, each as its own PR with behavior verified by driving the affected flow. Do NOT pursue (f) this round. Sequence suggestion: auth.js + pure-helpers + dedupe first (this round) → then AuxQueue and skill-sync (well-isolated) → then token-usage and provider-defaults → only then consider runChatTurn decomposition with dedicated e2e coverage.

verifies.reasoning: All 6 literals are REAL and verified verbatim: chat.js:2249, manage.js:1858/2051/5281/5292/5617 each contain the exact array ['opus','sonnet','haiku','fable']. The server-contract claim is also accurate: src/providers.js:293 hardcodes the 4 ANTHROPIC_DEFAULT_{OPUS,SONNET,HAIKU,FABLE}_MODEL env keys, and providers.js:305-309 derives aliasMap tiers from them — so the frontend literal is genuinely a duplicated contract. Severity LOW is correct (maintainability/DRY, not a functional bug; a missed site on a future tier add would desync editor vs picker). HOWEVER the recommendation as written — "Export const ALIAS_TIERS from the shared module (finding pure-alias-helpers-duplicated)" — is NOT independently applicable: chat.js and manage.js are loaded as plain <script src> tags (chat.html:1395, manage.html:1362) with no ES module system anywhere (no type="module", no import/export). There is no shared module to export from. Creating one would either introduce ES modules (high risk) or add a new .js + <script> tags in two HTML files outside the 4 target files (scope creep + load-order coupling). This finding is explicitly coupled to / blocked by the separate pure-alias-helpers-duplicated finding.
verifies.implementation_hint: Apply the safe in-scope variant, NOT the cross-file shared module. Define a per-file const once at the top of each file near the other alias helpers (e.g. manage.js around line 1854 just before providerAliasMap, and chat.js around line 2246): `const ALIAS_TIERS = ['opus', 'sonnet', 'haiku', 'fable'];` Then replace the 5 literals in manage.js (lines 1858, 2051, 5281, 5292, 5617) and the 1 in chat.js (line 2249) with references to ALIAS_TIERS. This is pure constant substitution: identical array contents, same iteration order, no new files, no HTML edits, no module system. Behavior is preserved byte-for-byte (the only difference is JS hoisting — `const` is block-scoped and not hoisted like `function`/`var`, so place the const ABOVE all 6 use sites at module top-level, not inside a function). Defer the cross-file shared module to the pure-alias-helpers-duplicated finding, which already needs to solve the no-module-system problem for the duplicated providerAliasTiers/normalizeModelForProvider helpers.

verifies.reasoning: Verified at public/manage.js:1855-1861 (providerAliasTiers) and public/manage.js:2049-2058 (rebuildModelOptions inline). The exact tier list `['opus','sonnet','haiku','fable']` plus predicate `.filter(t => map[t] && map[t].model)` is duplicated within the same file. The finding correctly identifies that rebuildModelOptions cannot simply call providerAliasTiers(providerId) because it reads aliasMap from a caller-passed `providers` array (prov = providers.find(...)), whereas providerAliasTiers reads from global _providerData.providers — so the map must be passed directly. Behavior is preserved by extracting aliasTiersFromMap(map) returning the richer pairs form [tier, entry]: providerAliasTiers becomes a one-line delegate, and rebuildModelOptions destructures ([t, m]) in its .map. Edge cases collapse identically (null map → [], empty {} → [], undefined prov → null → []). The aliasMap local is used ONLY for the tiers computation (verified by reading full function 2042-2079), so it can be dropped. In scope: touches only public/manage.js, no API/Flutter/schema change. Severity low is correct (small blast radius, UI dropdown). Note: the recommendation's "shared module" does not currently exist (aliasTiersFromMap appears nowhere in the repo) — implement as a LOCAL helper in manage.js this round, and leave the chat.js:2246 cross-file copy as a separate finding.

Verified by reading the exact ranges. Five of the six helpers are byte-identical between public/chat.js and public/manage.js: CLAUDE_MODEL_OPTIONS (chat.js:2075-2083 vs manage.js:1831-1839), modelShortName (chat.js:2104-2107 vs manage.js:1841-1844), providerAliasTiers (chat.js:2242-2248 vs manage.js:1855-1861), normalizeModelForProvider (chat.js:2252-2259 vs manage.js:1865-1872), and modelDisplayName (chat.js:2290-2304 vs manage.js:1876-1890, including the identical Chinese comments). providerAliasMap is genuinely divergent exactly as described: chat.js:2231-2237 reads `_providerList` and filters entries lacking `.model` (`Object.entries(p.aliasMap).filter(([,v]) => v && v.model)`), while manage.js:1848-1852 reads `_providerData.providers` and returns the raw aliasMap unfiltered — so the drift the finding warns about has already happened, concretely, not hypothetically. All four files are in the target list and the refactor touches no HTTP API, no Flutter code, no persistence schema. No `type="module"` exists anywhere (grep returned nothing), so the classic-script + globals pattern is correct; i18n.js is already loaded as a classic `<script>` and exposes the global `tt()` both pages call, so `shared/models.js` mirrors an established, working pattern. No `public/shared/` dir and no models.js/shared.js collision exist. All call sites (chat.js: 2115,2254,2264,2303,2359,2454; manage.js: 868,1092,1497,1867,1889,1907,1917,2276,3482) invoke these as top-level functions of (providerId[, model]) — so as long as each page keeps thin local wrappers, every call site stays valid and behavior is preserved. Severity medium is fair: pure frontend display logic (no data-loss surface), but the duplication is ~40 lines and the drift is already real.
verifies.implementation_hint: Create public/shared/models.js as a classic <script> (NOT an ES module) and add `<script src="shared/models.js"></script>` in chat.html between pwa.js (1394) and chat.js (1395), and in manage.html between pwa.js (1361) and manage.js (1362) — mirrors the i18n.js pattern exactly. IMPORTANT correction to the finding's wording: only CLAUDE_MODEL_OPTIONS and modelShortName can be moved verbatim. providerAliasTiers / normalizeModelForProvider / modelDisplayName CANNOT be moved verbatim because their bodies call providerAliasMap(providerId), which must stay page-local (chat.js reads `_providerList` + filters `.model`; manage.js reads `_providerData.providers` unfiltered). Instead, expose renamed shared functions that take the aliasMap OBJECT: aliasTiersFromMap(aliasMap) { if (!aliasMap) return []; return ['opus','sonnet','haiku','fable'].filter(t => aliasMap[t] && aliasMap[t].model).map(t => [t, aliasMap[t]]); }, normalizeModelForAlias(aliasMap, model) { if (!model) return model; for (const [t,m] of aliasTiersFromMap(aliasMap)) { if (t === model) return model; if (m.model === model) return t; } return model; }, modelDisplayNameFromMap(aliasMap, model) { if (!model) return model; if (aliasMap) { if (aliasMap[model]) { const e = aliasMap[model]; if (e.name) return e.name; if (e.model) return e.model; } for (const v of Object.values(aliasMap)) { if (v && v.model === model) return v.name || model; } } return modelShortName(model); }. Then in EACH page keep 1-line local wrappers that pass the page's own providerAliasMap result: providerAliasTiers(providerId) => aliasTiersFromMap(providerAliasMap(providerId)); normalizeModelForProvider(providerId, model) => normalizeModelForAlias(providerAliasMap(providerId), model); modelDisplayName(model, providerId) => modelDisplayNameFromMap(providerAliasMap(providerId), model). Delete the now-duplicated bodies of modelShortName + CLAUDE_MODEL_OPTIONS from both files. Leave providerAliasMap untouched in both files (it is the intentional page-specific boundary). Skip the finding's extra ALIAS_TIERS const and aliasTierLabel() — not currently duplicated (modelChoiceLabel exists only in chat.js), so adding them expands scope for no dedup benefit. Verify after: grep -n 'function providerAliasTiers\|function modelShortName\|function normalizeModelForProvider\|function modelDisplayName\|const CLAUDE_MODEL_OPTIONS' in each file shows only the intended thin wrappers / nothing duplicated.

verifies.reasoning: Every load-bearing claim verified against the code. (1) DEAD CODE: project-wide grep for `showModelPicker` in public/ finds exactly one hit in chat.js — the definition itself (worktree line 2165, main-repo target line 2169 as cited). No caller exists in chat.js, chat.html, or anywhere else; no eval/window[] indirect reference. The live model-button path is modelBtn → showAIConfigPicker (chat.js:2433), a different 3-field provider+model+effort dialog (chat.js:2306-2393). (2) LEGACY SIGNATURE: chat.js showModelPicker(current, providerOptions) consumes CLAUDE_MODEL_OPTIONS directly and is NOT alias-aware — it lacks providerAliasTiers/normalizeModelForProvider/buildModelChoices that manage.js's version (manage.js:1894-1969) and the live showAIConfigPicker both use. (3) MISSING FEATURES vs manage.js: chat.js's version has only ok/cancel/overlay onclick handlers; manage.js adds a keydown handler (Escape=reject, Enter=accept, manage.js:1959-1962) and setTimeout(()=>select.focus(),0) (manage.js:1967) — both absent in chat.js. (4) STRUCTURAL DUPE: overlay/box/select/custom-input/buttons chrome is ~40 near-identical lines. The finding's line range 2169-2224 matches the MAIN repo target file exactly; the current worktree branch sits 4 lines earlier (2165-2220) due to upstream edits, which is cosmetic. In scope (only public/chat.js), behavior-preserving (unreachable code), and the recommendation correctly scopes out the cross-file merge as MEDIUM risk. Severity 'medium' is slightly generous for inert dead code — corrected to 'low' — but the deletion is unambiguously worth doing this round.
verifies.implementation_hint: Delete the dead function and its preceding comment in public/chat.js. In the current worktree branch remove lines 2164-2220 inclusive: the comment `// WebView-safe picker (native select/confirm are unreliable in Android WebViews).` at 2164 plus the entire `function showModelPicker(current, providerOptions) { ... }` block (2165-2220). In the main repo target file the equivalent range is 2169-2224 (verify with `grep -n 'function showModelPicker' public/chat.js` immediately before editing, since line numbers drift across branches). Preserve the blank-line separation between the preceding showEffortPicker (ends with its closing `}`) and the following providerModelOptions helper. Before deleting, run a final guard grep: `grep -rn 'showModelPicker' public/` must show only the manage.js definition+call and the one chat.js definition — if any new chat.js caller appears, abort. Do NOT attempt to merge chat.js's showAIConfigPicker with manage.js's showModelPicker in this round (different field sets, DOM, keyboard, and focus behavior — MEDIUM risk per the finding).

verifies.reasoning: Confirmed dead code by direct inspection. src/providers.js:89-92 defines `function isAliasOnlyClaude(cfg)` returning `!!(env && env.ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL && !env.ANTHROPIC_MODEL)`. A repo-wide grep (server.js, src/, public/, tests/, scripts/, plugins/, docs/) finds the ONLY non-worktree occurrence is the definition line itself — zero call sites. It is not in `module.exports` (lines 824-843), so it cannot be invoked from server.js (`require('./src/providers')` at server.js:85) or anywhere else. The `aliasOnly` value is computed inline at src/providers.js:302 (`aliasOnly = !!baseUrl && !model`), :618 (console.log), and :620 (`aliasOnly: !!env.ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL && !src.ANTHROPIC_MODEL` in the returned object) — all three real callsites already cover the check without the helper. Deleting a never-invoked, never-exported function cannot change runtime behavior. In scope (src/providers.js is a target file), no API/Flutter/schema surface affected.
verifies.implementation_hint: Delete lines 87-92 of src/providers.js (the 2-line comment "// True for claude configs that need the wire-default remap..." + the 4-line `function isAliasOnlyClaude(cfg) { ... }` body). This leaves line 86 (`}` closing the previous function), the existing blank line 93, then the `// ── multicc store` section header at line 94 — clean spacing preserved. Verify after edit: `grep -rn isAliasOnlyClaude src/ server.js public/ tests/` returns empty, and `node -e "require('./src/providers')"` loads without error. No other files need touching.

verifies.reasoning: Verified at the cited lines. chat.js:2491 declares `let _providerList = [];` as a flat array of `{id,appType,name,baseUrl,model,isOfficial}`; it is populated by ensureProviderList(appType) (chat.js:2505-2513) which fetches `/api/providers?appType=<cli>` (FILTERED, line 2507) and stores ONLY `d.providers` (line 2510). manage.js:5241 declares `let _providerData = { available: false, providers: [], defaults: { claude: null, codex: null } };` — a full management payload; it is populated by loadProviders() (manage.js:5341-5357) which fetches `/api/providers` (UNFILTERED, line 5343) and stores the WHOLE response (line 5345), including `ccSwitchAvailable`, `defaults`, and `stats`. Read patterns differ too: chat.js does `_providerList.find(o => o.id === ...)` (lines 2228/2237/2495), manage.js touches `_providerData.providers` AND `_providerData.defaults[cli]` (5469/5543) AND `_providerData.ccSwitchAvailable` (5351/5353) AND `_providerData.stats` (5492). Even the duplicated `providerAliasMap` helper is NOT identical: chat.js:2235-2240 filters entries to those with a model (`v && v.model`), manage.js:1848-1851 uses `Object.keys(p.aliasMap).length` with no per-entry filter. So the divergence is real and intentional — a naive "extract everything provider-related" pass that merged these caches would force chat.js to carry defaults/ccSwitch/stats fields it never reads, change fetch lifecycles (per-appType filtered vs unfiltered full payload), and risk exactly the behavior change the hard constraints forbid. In scope (only public/chat.js + public/manage.js). No API/Flutter/schema impact.
verifies.implementation_hint: Skip as a standalone change for this round. The "problem" is not a defect — the design is already correct, so there is nothing functional to fix. The substantive recommendation ("do NOT merge the caches") is a guardrail/negative instruction whose real value is as implementation guidance for the RELATED pure-alias-helpers-duplicated finding, not a change to apply here. The only positive action this finding prescribes is "document this so future refactors don't fold them together" — i.e. a 2-line comment, which is cosmetic and falls outside the "LOW-RISK, HIGH-VALUE" preference. If anything is done, the maximum safe action is two one-line comments: at chat.js:2491 append `// page-local: do NOT merge with manage.js _providerData (different shape/lifecycle)` and at manage.js:5241 append `// page-local full mgmt payload; chat.js keeps its own flat _providerList by design`. But that is genuinely optional. The important thing is that whatever implements pure-alias-helpers-duplicated exposes pure functions taking a provider/aliasMap OBJECT (so each page keeps its own cache + its own ~2-line `providerAliasMap` lookup) — fold THIS guardrail into that finding's implementation_hint rather than treating it as a standalone refactor.

verifies.reasoning: The core duplication is REAL and verified byte-for-byte. public/chat.js:2282 returns `${v}${m.name ? ` · ${m.name}` : ''} · ${m.model}` inside modelChoiceLabel; public/manage.js:1912 inlines the identical template inside showModelPicker's optionList; public/manage.js:2057 inlines it again inside rebuildModelOptions. So a label-format change would indeed need 3 edits. However, two of the finding's justifications are wrong. (1) The XSS framing is incorrect: all three labels are consumed via opt.textContent assignment (chat.js:2369, manage.js:1923, manage.js:2066), never innerHTML. textContent never parses HTML, so there is no injection surface and no 'XSS fix must land 3x' concern. (2) The recommendation 'extract aliasTierLabel into the shared module' rests on a non-existent premise: there is no shared module between chat.js and manage.js. Every adjacent helper (providerAliasMap at chat.js:2235/manage.js:1848, providerAliasTiers at chat.js:2246/manage.js:1855, modelShortName, escapeHtml) is ALREADY duplicated across these two files. So a true shared-module extraction would require introducing a brand-new <script>/load-order dependency — disproportionate for a one-line format string and inconsistent with the existing architecture. The only behavior-preserving, low-risk path is per-file extraction (matching the established duplication pattern), which reduces 3 inline copies to 2 helper definitions (one per file) — the format literal would still live twice. That is a marginal, cosmetic win.
verifies.implementation_hint: Skip this round: low value (cosmetic dedup of a one-line template), the strongest stated motivation (XSS) is wrong since all consumers use textContent, and the recommended 'shared module' does not exist. If still desired later, the only safe approach is per-file extraction matching the existing duplicated-helper pattern: in manage.js add `function aliasTierLabel(t, m){ return `${t}${m.name ? ` · ${m.name}` : ''} · ${m.model}`; }` next to providerAliasTiers (~line 1858) and replace both inline uses at 1912 and 2057; in chat.js add the same helper near providerAliasTiers (~line 2253) and replace the inline at 2282. Do NOT add escapeHtml at any site — that would double-encode the label into textContent and visibly change behavior (e.g. '&' artifacts). No API/schema/Flutter impact.

verifies.reasoning: Verified by reading src/providers.js:350-365 and :820-843, plus repo-wide grep. Line 364-365 defines `const getProviderRow = getProvider;` under a comment that explicitly says it exists because "server.js used getProviderRow previously." Line 829 exports it. A repo-wide grep for `getProviderRow` excluding node_modules, sibling worktree copies, and providers.js itself returns ZERO hits — server.js, public/chat.js, public/manage.js, and tests have no references. server.js:85 requires ./src/providers and uses only providers.getProvider / providers.getProviderSummary / providers.getProviderUsageStats (confirmed at server.js:517,527,609,1874,2587,4420,4611,4709,6762). The back-compat consumer no longer exists, so both the alias and its export are dead. In scope (touches only src/providers.js, one of the 4 target files; no API/Flutter/schema change). Behavior preserved: getProviderRow is a pure alias of getProvider with no callers, so removal changes nothing at runtime; getProvider remains exported and used.
verifies.implementation_hint: Two deletions in src/providers.js: (1) remove lines 364-365 (the comment `// Back-compat alias (server.js used getProviderRow previously).` and `const getProviderRow = getProvider;`); (2) in the module.exports block, remove the single line `  getProviderRow,` (currently line 829, between `getProvider,` and `getProviderSummary,`). After deletion, optionally confirm with `node -e "const p=require('./src/providers'); console.log(typeof p.getProvider, typeof p.getProviderRow)"` — getProvider should print 'function', getProviderRow 'undefined'. No test changes needed since nothing references the alias.
